title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text: Analytical work requires two foundational capabilities: understanding how data is structured and stored, and knowing how to retrieve and manipulate it computationally. This lecture covers scales of measurement, database structure, proxy variables, and data quality — the structural foundations — then introduces the computational tools that operationalize those concepts: SQL for querying relational databases and APIs for programmatic data collection.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Learning Objectives

Explain the basic concepts of probability, including sample spaces, events, and probability measures. Demonstrate the ability to calculate probabilities for simple events and their complements.
Apply the rules of probability, such as the addition rule and multiplication rule, to solve probability problems involving combined events and conditional probabilities.
Develop the skill to identify real-world scenarios where probabilistic thinking is applicable. Apply probability concepts to analyze uncertain situations, make informed decisions, and assess risks.
Introduce Bayesian reasoning and its application in probabilistic modeling. Understand the process of Bayesian updating and its significance in data-driven decision-making.
Describe the data science process from data collection to model deployment. Identify the key steps involved, including data exploration, data preprocessing, modeling, and evaluation.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
The Five Scales




Nominal Scale

An unordered set of categories identified only by name or label. Numbers, if used, have no mathematical meaning. You can only determine sameness or difference. Examples: country names, ethnic group, religious affiliation, vehicle type. Valid operations: counting, mode, frequency tables, chi-square tests.



Ordinal Scale

An ordered set of categories. You know the direction of difference (A > B) but not the magnitude — the intervals between ranks are not assumed equal. Examples: military rank (Private < Corporal < Sergeant), education level, Likert survey responses (Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree), threat level (Low / Medium / High). Valid operations: median, percentiles, rank-order correlations (Spearman’s rho).



Interval Scale

An ordered scale with equal intervals between values, but no true zero point. Zero is arbitrary — it does not mean “absence” of the attribute. Examples: temperature in Celsius or Fahrenheit (0°C does not mean “no temperature”), IQ scores, calendar years. Valid operations: mean, standard deviation, Pearson correlation. Ratios are not valid (40°C is not “twice as hot” as 20°C).



Ratio Scale

An interval scale with a true, meaningful zero — zero indicates complete absence of the attribute. All arithmetic operations including ratios are valid. Examples: weight, income, distance, duration, number of casualties, GDP in dollars.



Binary Scale

A special case of nominal data with exactly two values, often coded 0/1. Examples: treaty membership (Yes/No), conflict onset (1 = conflict this year, 0 = no conflict), gender in binary operationalizations. Also called dummy variables in regression analysis.











TipA Quick Memory Aid: NOIR



Nominal → Ordinal → Interval → Ratio. Each level includes the properties of the one before it. Ratio data gives you the most information; nominal gives you the least.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
What is a Database?
A database is an organized, structured collection of data that enables efficient storage, retrieval, updating, and management. Every IC data platform — from classified warehouses to commercial analytics tools like Palantir — is built on relational database principles.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Tables, Fields, and Records
Every relational database is built from tables:








Concept
Definition
Analogy




Table
A collection of related data organized in rows and columns
A spreadsheet tab


Field (Column)
A single data attribute; the variable being recorded
A column header


Record (Row)
A single observation or entry
One row of data









NoteThe “Tall Rectangle” Rule



Well-structured databases are tall (many rows, fewer columns), not wide (few rows, many columns). Each row is one observation; each column is one variable. Violating this — putting “Year 2020 GDP,” “Year 2021 GDP,” “Year 2022 GDP” as three separate columns — makes joining, querying, and analysis far more difficult. This is the tidy data principle.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Database Keys


Primary Key
A field (or combination of fields) that uniquely identifies each record in a table. Requirements: - Must be unique — no two rows can share the same value - Cannot be NULL (empty) - Should be stable — should not change over time
Examples: Student ID, UN country code, a system-generated UUID.


Foreign Key
A field in one table that references the primary key in another table. Foreign keys enforce referential integrity — you cannot create a record referencing an entity that does not exist in the parent table.
Purpose: links tables together, enabling relational queries. A “Conflict Events” table with a country_id foreign key links to a “Countries” table, enabling country-level aggregation.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Structuring Data for Analysis
Best practices when building or receiving a dataset:

Use unique identifiers in the first column
Normalize data: break related information into logical separate tables rather than repeating values
Use consistent naming conventions — never mix USA, U.S., and United States in the same column
Use appropriate data types — store numbers as numbers, dates as dates, not as text
Document every field in a data dictionary (see Part X)

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
What is a Proxy Variable?
A proxy variable is a variable used in place of one that cannot be directly measured. Many of the most important concepts in intelligence — state intentions, regime stability, radicalization, military readiness — cannot be directly observed. Analysts must substitute measurable indicators.






NoteExamples of Proxy Variables in IA










Concept (Unobservable)
Proxy Variable (Observable)




Nuclear weapons capability
Number of centrifuges; plutonium production reactor capacity


State instability
GDP per capita growth, infant mortality rate, Fragile States Index score


Regime intent to proliferate
Imports of dual-use materials; missile test frequency


Social media radicalization
Proportion of followers linked to designated extremist accounts


Military readiness
Training exercise frequency; logistics vehicle activity in satellite imagery

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Cautions in Using Proxies
When using proxy variables, always:

Justify the connection — explain theoretically why your proxy measures the underlying concept
Acknowledge the gap — the proxy is not the concept; be explicit about what it misses
Check for alternative explanations — could your proxy be driven by something other than the concept you care about?
Use multiple proxies — triangulation across several imperfect indicators is stronger than reliance on one

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Why Is Data “Dirty”?
Real-world data is almost never clean. It arrives from multiple sources, collected at different times, by different people, using different standards. The preprocessing and cleaning step — often 60–80% of total project time — is where analytical quality is won or lost. Errors introduced here compound through every subsequent step.



Missing Values

Fields with no recorded value. Can occur because the data was never collected, was collected but lost, is being withheld, or represents a genuine non-occurrence. How missing values are handled is one of the most consequential methodological choices in any analysis.

Duplicate Records

The same entity or event appearing more than once in the dataset, usually as a result of merging data from multiple sources. Duplicates inflate counts and distort averages.

Inconsistent Coding

The same concept encoded differently across records: “USA,” “U.S.A.,” “United States,” “us.” Even minor inconsistencies break joins and aggregations.

Measurement Error

Recorded values that differ from true values due to instrument error, rounding, transcription mistakes, or deliberate falsification. State-reported economic data and casualty counts are common examples.

Structural Zeros

A value of zero that is not missing but represents a genuine absence. A dataset of conflict events may show zero events in a country not because data is unavailable, but because no events occurred.

Outliers

Extreme values that may represent genuine anomalies, data entry errors, or natural variation. Require investigation before any decision about handling.










WarningThe Intelligence-Specific Data Quality Problem



In intelligence contexts, data quality problems are often not random — they are the result of deliberate deception (adversaries reporting false numbers), selection bias (data only exists where collection infrastructure exists), or classification (the most critical information may be unavailable). These systematic biases cannot be corrected through statistical imputation. They must be reasoned about explicitly.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
section: Not All Missing Data Is the Same
text:
Not All Missing Data Is the Same
The statistical literature identifies three distinct mechanisms for missing data, each with different implications for how it should be handled:



Missing Completely at Random (MCAR)

The probability that a value is missing is unrelated to any variable in the dataset — not to the missing value itself, nor to other observed values. Example: a random sample of survey responses lost in a server crash. MCAR is the most benign assumption; listwise deletion (removing incomplete cases) does not introduce bias, though it reduces sample size.

Missing at Random (MAR)

The probability of being missing is related to observed variables, but not to the missing value itself, after conditioning on those variables. Example: older survey respondents are less likely to answer questions about internet use, but among respondents of the same age, missingness is random. Multiple imputation or maximum likelihood estimation can handle MAR data without serious bias.

Missing Not at Random (MNAR)

The probability of being missing depends on the missing value itself. Example: states with the highest human rights violations are the least likely to allow independent human rights monitoring — so the countries with the worst records are precisely the ones missing from the dataset. This is the most serious case; no statistical procedure can fully correct for MNAR without modeling the missing data mechanism explicitly.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
The Intelligence Application






WarningMNAR is the Default Assumption for Intelligence Data



In intelligence analysis, the assumption that data is MCAR or even MAR is almost always wrong. The most sensitive facilities are the least surveilled. The most dangerous individuals take the most precautions to avoid detection. The most repressive governments produce the least reliable official statistics. Data that is systematically missing because of the very phenomenon you are studying is the rule, not the exception. Analysts who impute missing values as if they were MCAR introduce exactly the kind of bias that makes analysis misleading precisely in the highest-stakes cases.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Common Handling Approaches (and their limits)









Approach
Assumption Required
When Appropriate
Risks




Listwise deletion
MCAR
When missing data is truly random and sample is large
Biased if MAR or MNAR; reduces power


Mean/median imputation
MCAR
Exploratory work only
Artificially reduces variance; distorts distributions


Multiple imputation
MAR
When missingness can be predicted from observed variables
Cannot correct MNAR


Model the missingness
MNAR explicitly modeled
When you have a theory of why values are missing
Complex; requires strong assumptions


Sensitivity analysis
Any
When you are uncertain about the mechanism
Tests whether conclusions are robust to alternative assumptions

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
section: The Most Value-Adding Step in Analytical Work
text:
The Most Value-Adding Step in Analytical Work
Data integration — combining information from multiple sources into a unified analytical dataset — is where the greatest analytical value is created and where the greatest analytical errors are introduced. No single source answers complex intelligence questions. The ability to merge, align, and reconcile disparate datasets is a core analytical skill.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Integration in the Intelligence Community
The IC operates from a collection philosophy of all-source analysis: HUMINT, SIGINT, IMINT, GEOINT, OSINT, and MASINT all provide partial pictures that only make sense when fused. The transition from single-source reporting to all-source assessment is the integration problem in its most consequential form.
At the data level, integration requires:

A common key to link records across datasets (country code, person identifier, geographic coordinate, date)
Consistent units and scales (all monetary figures in the same currency and year; all dates in the same format)
Temporal alignment (data collected at different frequencies or with different lags requires aggregation or interpolation)
Level-of-analysis compatibility (country-level data cannot be merged with individual-level data without aggregation decisions that embed analytical judgment)

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Why Integration Is Difficult



No Common Key

Datasets from different organizations often lack a shared unique identifier. Matching “Abu Ahmad al-Iraqi” across three databases that each use a different transliteration scheme and ID system is a non-trivial entity resolution problem.

Definitional Divergence

Two datasets measuring “conflict events” may define them differently — one includes protests, the other does not. Merging them requires reconciling the definitional gap, which is an analytical judgment, not a technical procedure.

Temporal Misalignment

ACLED releases data weekly; UCDP annually; World Bank GDP estimates may lag by 18 months. Integrating these requires decisions about what time period each variable represents.

Level-of-Analysis Mismatch

Merging individual-level survey data with country-level economic statistics requires aggregating one or disaggregating the other — each approach discards information and risks the ecological fallacy.

Entity Resolution at Scale

When integrating large datasets, the same real-world entity may appear under dozens of name variants, typos, or aliases. Automated fuzzy matching (edit distance, phonetic matching) helps but always introduces false positives and false negatives.

Provenance Loss

Once data from multiple sources is merged, the trail back to original sources becomes harder to maintain. A merged dataset with no provenance documentation cannot be audited, replicated, or corrected.

Changing Coverage

A dataset that covered 50 countries in 2005 and 80 in 2020 will show apparent change that reflects coverage expansion, not real-world change. Integration across time requires attention to whether coverage is constant.










WarningThe Invisible Error Problem



The most dangerous errors in data integration are the ones that produce no obvious error messages. A join that silently drops unmatched records, a merge that produces many-to-many duplications, a temporal alignment that misassigns observations to the wrong period — all of these can produce analytically plausible results that are systematically wrong. Spot-checking merged data against known cases and original sources is essential validation.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Problem: Understanding Future Energy Markets
Working with those near you: identify 10 variables you would include in a model of future energy markets. For each variable, specify:

The variable name
Its type (quantitative/qualitative) and scale of measurement (nominal/ordinal/interval/ratio/binary)
The data source you would use to operationalize it
Whether it is a direct measure or a proxy, and if a proxy, what concept it is proxying

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
section: Case 1: Measuring Democracy — A Measurement War
text:
Case 1: Measuring Democracy — A Measurement War
“Democracy” is one of the most consequential variables in international relations research — and one of the most contested to operationalize. Three major indices operationalize the same concept in fundamentally different ways and regularly produce conflicting results.










Index
Producer
Approach
Scale
Known Issues




Polity V
Center for Systemic Peace
Codes executive recruitment, constraints on executive, and political competition
−10 (autocracy) to +10 (democracy)
Coders sometimes disagree significantly; transitions poorly captured


Freedom House
Freedom House (U.S. NGO)
Rates political rights and civil liberties separately; explicitly normative framing
1–7 on each dimension; aggregate Free/Partly Free/Not Free
Critics argue ratings are influenced by U.S. foreign policy interests


V-Dem
V-Dem Institute, University of Gothenburg
Multi-dimensional: measures electoral, liberal, participatory, deliberative, and egalitarian democracy separately
0–1 on each component index
Less tested for very recent events









WarningMunck and Verkuilen’s Critique



In a widely-cited 2002 article, Munck and Verkuilen evaluated twelve democracy indices on conceptualization, measurement, and aggregation. They found that no existing index performed well on all three dimensions — each had significant methodological vulnerabilities. Any analyst using a democracy index should read this critique before treating scores as ground truth.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
section: Case 2: Conflict Counting — ACLED vs. UCDP
text:
Case 2: Conflict Counting — ACLED vs. UCDP


ACLED

Unit: individual events (battles, violence, protests, riots)
Threshold: any organized political violence — no minimum fatality threshold
Strength: captures low-intensity conflict; near real-time
Limitation: over-represents media-accessible areas



UCDP

Unit: conflict dyads (organized actor vs. organized actor)
Threshold: 25 battle-related deaths per year minimum
Strength: rigorous definitional consistency; long historical coverage (1946–present)
Limitation: misses sub-threshold violence; annual release cycle



Studies using UCDP will show fewer conflicts than studies using ACLED for the same country-year. Neither is “wrong” — they measure different things. An analyst must select based on whether their question is about organized armed conflict above a fatality threshold or any politically motivated violence.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
What Is a Data Dictionary?
A data dictionary documents every field in a dataset: its name, data type, allowed values, definition, and source. Without it, a dataset is almost impossible to use correctly — especially when received from another organization.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
section: Example: A Conflict Events Data Dictionary
text:
Example: A Conflict Events Data Dictionary










Field Name
Data Type
Scale
Allowed Values
Definition




event_id
Integer
—
Unique integer
System-generated primary key


country_code
String (3)
Nominal
ISO alpha-3
ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country code


event_date
Date
Interval
YYYY-MM-DD
Date the event occurred (UTC)


event_type
String
Nominal
Battle / VAC / Protest / Riot / Explosion
Type of political violence


fatalities
Integer
Ratio
0–99999; −9 = unknown
Reported deaths; −9 if unknown


latitude
Float
Ratio
−90.0 to 90.0
WGS84 latitude of event location


longitude
Float
Ratio
−180.0 to 180.0
WGS84 longitude of event location









NoteThe Data Dictionary as an Analytical Contract



When you receive data from a partner agency or academic database, the data dictionary defines what you are actually getting. Missing or ambiguous dictionaries are among the most common sources of analytical error.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Spreadsheets
Spreadsheets (Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets) are the most accessible computational tool: a visual grid for entering, cleaning, computing, and charting small tabular datasets, with formulas and pivot tables for quick summaries. Their strength is immediacy; their weakness is scale and reproducibility. As the Reinhart–Rogoff error (this week’s required reading) demonstrates, an unseen formula mistake in a spreadsheet can propagate silently into a consequential conclusion. Spreadsheets do not scale to millions of rows and leave no clear audit trail of what was done.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Relational Databases and SQL
A relational database stores structured data across linked tables (Part II). SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language for retrieving and combining that data. Rather than scrolling through a table, the analyst describes the records they want and the database returns them.
SELECT country, year, battle_deaths
FROM   conflict_events
WHERE  region = 'Sahel'
  AND  year >= 2015
ORDER BY battle_deaths DESC;
This query asks for three fields, filtered to one region and time window, sorted by severity. A JOIN combines tables on a shared key — for example, attaching economic indicators to each conflict record:
SELECT e.country, e.battle_deaths, c.gdp_per_capita
FROM   conflict_events AS e
JOIN   country_indicators AS c
  ON   e.country = c.country AND e.year = c.year
WHERE  e.year = 2020;
SQL is how analysts work with IC data warehouses and large relational datasets — it turns a structural relationship between tables (Part II’s keys) into an analytical result.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Programming Languages
For anything beyond a single query — statistical modeling, automation, reproducible workflows, machine learning — analysts turn to general-purpose languages. R (with the tidyverse and ggplot2) is built for statistics, exploratory data analysis, and visualization. Python (with pandas, NumPy, and scikit-learn) is a general-purpose language strong in automation, machine learning, text processing, and API access. Julia targets high-performance numerical computing. Their shared advantage over spreadsheets is reproducibility: the analysis is written as code that can be reviewed, rerun, and audited — the computational embodiment of the data dictionary’s “analytical contract” (Part X).

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)
An API is a defined interface that lets one program request data or services from another. Instead of manually downloading files, an analyst sends a structured request to a remote system and receives structured data — usually JSON — in return.
GET https://api.worldbank.org/v2/country/IR/indicator/NY.GDP.MKTP.CD?format=json
This request retrieves Iran’s GDP series from the World Bank’s public API. APIs are the backbone of programmatic OSINT collection (Week 2): social-media platforms, financial data providers, flight- and vessel-tracking services, and government open-data portals all expose APIs that let analysts pull current data automatically and repeatably rather than by hand.

title: Week 6: Data Structures, Databases, and Computational Tools
text:
Software Discussed in This Course
The table below inventories the major tools introduced across IA 240 — what each does, the kind of data it operates on, and where it appears in the course.









Software / Tool
What It Does
Type of Data It Uses
Example in This Course




Microsoft Excel
Enter, clean, compute, and chart small datasets; pivot tables
Small structured/tabular data
Reasoning about the NFL receiver dataset (Week 4); drafting a data dictionary


SQL (SQLite, PostgreSQL)
Query and join records in relational databases
Structured relational tables with keys
Filtering and joining conflict-event tables (this week)


R (tidyverse, ggplot2)
Statistical computing, EDA, modeling, visualization
Structured/tabular data; text
Descriptive statistics and hypothesis testing (Week 7); regression (Week 8)


Python (pandas, scikit-learn)
General-purpose analysis, automation, ML, API calls
Structured, text, and image data
Machine-learning classification and NLP (Week 9)


Julia
High-performance numerical and scientific computing
Numeric/structured data
Listed among core languages for computation at scale (Week 3)


NetLogo
Agent-based modeling environment
Agent rules + synthetic environment
Schelling segregation and insurgency ABMs (Week 10)


Mesa / Repast
Code frameworks for larger agent-based models
Agent rules / synthetic data
Building an ABM in Python or Java (Week 10)


Gephi
Network visualization and metrics (centrality, communities)
Network/graph data (nodes & edges)
Visualizing a covert network (Week 11)


NetworkX / igraph / statnet
Programmatic social network analysis
Network/graph data
Computing centrality on the 9/11 network (Week 11)


Maltego
OSINT link analysis and entity mapping
Entities and relationships (OSINT)
Mapping relationships from open sources (Week 11)


Palantir Gotham
Enterprise data integration; network and timeline analysis
Fused multi-source (structured + unstructured)
IC-scale integration and link analysis (Weeks 9, 11)


i2 Analyst’s Notebook
Link and timeline charting for investigations
Entities, relationships, events
Charting relationships and time-slicing a network (Week 11)


GIS (ArcGIS, QGIS)
Store, analyze, and map spatial data; overlay and spatial statistics
Geospatial vector and raster data
Hot-spot analysis of conflict events (Week 12)


Google Earth
Explore imagery and geospatial data; geolocation
Satellite/aerial imagery + geospatial data
Open-source geolocation of a photo (Weeks 2, 12)









TipA Pattern Worth Noticing



Read down the “Type of Data” column and the course’s logic reappears: the scale of measurement and structure of your data (Parts I–II) largely dictate which tool is appropriate. Tabular data goes to spreadsheets, databases, R, or Python; relational data to SQL; network data to Gephi or NetworkX; spatial data to GIS. Choosing a tool is, in part, a statement about what kind of data you believe you have.
